annotations.label: Statistics

anchor.snippet: is the discipline that concerns the collection, organization, analysis, interpretation, and presentation of data

annotations.note: A description of the discipline of statistics, not a mathematical statement formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Central tendency and dispersion

anchor.snippet: central tendency (or location ) seeks to characterize the distribution's central or typical value

annotations.note: Dispersion is captured by `variance` and central tendency by the expectation integral `μ[X]`, but no unified notion of central tendency/dispersion is defined.

annotations.label: Statistic (mean and dispersion)

anchor.snippet: Two elementary summaries of data, singularly called a statistic, are the mean and dispersion

annotations.note: Mean (expectation integral) and dispersion (`variance`) exist as formalized quantities, but not the informal summary-statistic concept.

annotations.label: Representative sampling

anchor.snippet: Representative sampling ensures that inferences and conclusions can safely be extended from the sample to the population as a whole

annotations.note: An informal methodological notion with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Sampling theory

anchor.snippet: Sampling theory is part of the mathematical discipline of probability theory

annotations.note: Describes a sub-discipline rather than a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Experimental study

anchor.snippet: An experimental study involves taking measurements of the system under study, manipulating the system

annotations.note: A methodological definition with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Observational study

anchor.snippet: an observational study does not involve experimental manipulation

annotations.note: A methodological definition with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Basic steps of a statistical experiment

anchor.snippet: The basic steps of a statistical experiment are

annotations.note: A procedural checklist, not a mathematical object.

annotations.label: Hawthorne study

anchor.snippet: The famous Hawthorne study examined changes to the working environment at the Hawthorne plant

annotations.note: A real-world case study with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Hawthorne effect

anchor.snippet: The Hawthorne effect refers to finding that an outcome

annotations.note: An empirical behavioral phenomenon, not formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Smoking and lung cancer observational study

anchor.snippet: An example of an observational study is one that explores the association between smoking and lung cancer

annotations.note: An applied example with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Case-control study

anchor.snippet: A case-control study is another type of observational study in which people with and without the outcome of interest

annotations.note: A study-design definition with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Nominal measurements

anchor.snippet: Nominal measurements do not have meaningful rank order among values

annotations.note: A measurement-scale taxonomy, not a formalized mathematical structure in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ordinal measurements

anchor.snippet: Ordinal measurements have imprecise differences between consecutive values, but have a meaningful order to those values

annotations.note: Though Mathlib has order structures, the statistical measurement-scale notion is not formalized.

annotations.label: Interval measurements

anchor.snippet: Interval measurements have meaningful distances between measurements defined, but the zero value is arbitrary

annotations.note: A measurement-scale taxonomy with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Ratio measurements

anchor.snippet: Ratio measurements have both a meaningful zero value and the distances between different measurements defined

annotations.note: A measurement-scale taxonomy with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Categorical and quantitative variables

anchor.snippet: sometimes they are grouped together as categorical variables

annotations.note: A data-typing distinction, not a formalized mathematical concept.

annotations.label: Descriptive statistic

anchor.snippet: A descriptive statistic (in the count noun sense) is a summary statistic that quantitatively describes or summarizes features of a collection of information

annotations.note: The general notion of a descriptive/summary statistic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Statistical inference

anchor.snippet: Statistical inference is the process of using data analysis to deduce properties of an underlying probability distribution

annotations.note: An overarching process description with no single Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Random sample

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: defines a random sample as the random vector given by the column vector of these IID variables

annotations.note: The IID notion underlying a random sample exists via `IdentDistrib` and `iIndepFun`, but no packaged 'random sample' object is defined.

annotations.label: Statistic

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: A statistic is a random variable that is a function of the random sample, but not a function of unknown parameters

annotations.note: Measurable functions of random variables exist generically, but the specific notion of a 'statistic' is not formalized.

annotations.label: Estimator

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: an estimator is a statistic used to estimate such function

annotations.note: Estimators are modeled as Markov kernels within the decision-theoretic risk framework (`avgRisk`/`bayesRisk`), but no standalone 'estimator' definition exists.

annotations.label: Pivotal quantity

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: whose probability distribution does not depend on the unknown parameter is called a pivotal quantity or pivot

annotations.note: No formalization of pivotal quantities in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Efficient estimator

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: the one with lower mean squared error is said to be more efficient

annotations.note: Estimator efficiency/MSE comparison is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unbiased and asymptotically unbiased estimator

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: an estimator is said to be unbiased if its expected value is equal to the true value of the unknown parameter being estimated

annotations.note: No notion of estimator (un)biasedness is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: UMVUE and consistent estimators

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: UMVUE estimators that have the lowest variance for all possible values of the parameter to be estimated

annotations.note: No formalization of UMVUE or estimator consistency in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Null hypothesis

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: the development of a null hypothesis which is usually (but not necessarily) that no relationship exists among variables

annotations.note: Hypothesis testing is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Alternative hypothesis

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: The alternative hypothesis is the name of the hypothesis that contradicts the null hypothesis

annotations.note: Hypothesis testing is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Criminal trial illustration

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: The best illustration for a novice is the predicament encountered by a criminal trial

annotations.note: A pedagogical analogy with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Type I and Type II errors

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: two broad categories of error are recognized

annotations.note: Type I/II error notions from hypothesis testing are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Standard deviation and standard error

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: Standard deviation refers to the extent to which individual observations in a sample differ from a central value

annotations.note: Variance is formalized (`variance`/`evariance`); standard deviation as its square root and standard error of an estimator are not separately defined.

annotations.label: Statistical error

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: A statistical error is the amount by which an observation differs from its expected value

annotations.note: The statistical error/residual decomposition is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Residual

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: A residual is the amount an observation differs from the value the estimator of the expected value assumes on a given sample

annotations.note: No formalization of regression/estimation residuals in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mean squared error

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: Mean squared error is used for obtaining efficient estimators

annotations.note: Variance (mean squared deviation from the expectation) is formalized and closely related, but MSE of an estimator is not defined as such.

annotations.label: Root mean square error

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: Root mean square error is simply the square root of mean squared error

annotations.note: RMSE is not defined in Mathlib (no MSE/RMSE estimator-error definitions).

annotations.label: Methods of least squares

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: Many statistical methods seek to minimize the residual sum of squares

annotations.note: The least-squares method/residual-sum-of-squares minimization is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ordinary and non-linear least squares

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: Least squares applied to linear regression is called ordinary least squares method

annotations.note: Ordinary and non-linear least squares regression are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Measurement error

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: Measurement processes that generate statistical data are also subject to error

annotations.note: Measurement error (random vs. systematic) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Confidence interval

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: a 95% confidence interval for a value is a range where, if the sampling and analysis were repeated under the same conditions

annotations.note: Confidence intervals are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Credible interval

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: is to use a credible interval from Bayesian statistics

annotations.note: Bayesian credible intervals are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Statistical significance and p-value

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: often refers to the probability of a value accurately rejecting the null hypothesis

annotations.note: Statistical significance and p-values are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Critical region

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: A critical region is the set of values of the estimator that leads to refuting the null hypothesis

annotations.note: The critical/rejection region of a test is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Statistical power

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: The statistical power of a test is the probability that it correctly rejects the null hypothesis when the null hypothesis is false

annotations.note: Statistical power of a test is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Significance level

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: the significance level is the largest p-value that allows the test to reject the null hypothesis

annotations.note: The significance level of a test is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: p-value (formal)

anchor.section: Terminology and theory of inferential statistics
anchor.snippet: the p-value is the probability, assuming the null hypothesis is true, of observing a result at least as extreme as the test statistic

annotations.note: The formal p-value definition is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bayesian updating

anchor.snippet: is to use Bayes' theorem to update the prior probability of the hypotheses in consideration based on the relative likelihood of the evidence gathered

annotations.note: Bayes' theorem itself is formalized (`cond_eq_inv_mul_cond_mul`, plus a measure-kernel posterior form), but the Bayesian-updating procedure over hypotheses is not packaged.

annotations.label: Hierarchical Bayesian model (YouTube views)

anchor.snippet: one could model each YouTube channel as having video views distributed as a normal distribution with channel dependent mean and variance

annotations.note: Gaussian distributions exist in Mathlib, but no hierarchical Bayesian model construction is formalized.

annotations.label: Exploratory data analysis

anchor.snippet: Exploratory data analysis ( EDA ) is an approach to analyzing data sets to summarize their main characteristics

annotations.note: EDA is a methodological approach with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Mathematical statistics

anchor.snippet: Mathematical statistics is the application of mathematics to statistics

annotations.note: Describes a sub-discipline rather than a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Stratified vs purposive sampling (Neyman)

anchor.snippet: stratified random sampling was in general a better method of estimation than purposive (quota) sampling

annotations.note: Neyman's comparison of sampling methods is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Applied statistics

anchor.section: Applied statistics, theoretical statistics and mathematical statistics
anchor.snippet: comprises descriptive statistics and the application of inferential statistics

annotations.note: A disciplinary description, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Theoretical statistics

anchor.section: Applied statistics, theoretical statistics and mathematical statistics
anchor.snippet: Theoretical statistics concerns the logical arguments underlying justification of approaches to statistical inference

annotations.note: A disciplinary description, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Machine learning models

anchor.snippet: Machine learning models are statistical and probabilistic models that capture patterns in the data through use of computational algorithms

annotations.note: Machine learning models are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Econometrics

anchor.snippet: Econometrics is the application of statistical methods to economic data in order to give empirical content to economic relationships

annotations.note: An applied field description with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Statistical literacy

anchor.snippet: is referred to as statistical literacy

annotations.note: A social/educational concept, not formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Income and age of death correlation

anchor.snippet: a study of annual income that also looks at age of death, might find that poor people tend to have shorter lives than affluent people

annotations.note: An illustrative example with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Lurking (confounding) variable

anchor.snippet: called a lurking variable or confounding variable

annotations.note: Confounding/lurking variables are a causal-inference concept not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Correlation does not imply causation

anchor.snippet: there is no way to immediately infer the existence of a causal relationship between the two variables

annotations.note: Mathlib defines covariance but not correlation or any causal-inference framework, so this principle is not formalized.
